title: Kali, Durga, and the Spiritual Permission of Rage
dek: You have been told that your anger is unspiritual, a low vibration to be cleared. But the ancient lineages knew better. When the jaw tightens and the psoas contracts, you are not failing at enlightenment - you are remembering the fierce, protective deities that live in your own fascia.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Myth of the Placid Saint


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.224Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You sit on the meditation cushion, trying to breathe into the belly, trying to let the thoughts pass like clouds. But your jaw is clamped so tight the masseter muscle aches. Your shoulders are hitched up around your ears, the trapezius tight as a bowstring. You are trying to be spiritual. You are trying to be good. You have been told, perhaps by a yoga teacher with a very soft voice, that anger is a low vibration. That it is something to be cleared, cleansed, released, or transcended. 

But the body knows. The body remembers. The sudden heat in the chest, the tightening of the throat, the way the breath becomes shallow and sharp - this is not a failure of your spiritual practice. This is biology. This is the ancient, protective architecture of your nervous system waking up to say: *Something is wrong here. Something must be protected.*

When we look at the iconography of the divine feminine in many ancient traditions, we do not only see placid, smiling mothers. We see Kali, tongue out, wearing a garland of skulls, dancing on the chest of Shiva. We see Durga, riding a lion, armed with weapons in all her many hands, created specifically to defeat a demon that no male god could touch. These are not metaphors for a polite, sanitized kind of boundary-setting. They are the spiritual permission of rage.

## The Anatomy of Fury

Let us look at what actually happens in the body when anger arises. It is not a conceptual event. It is a deeply somatic one. The amygdala registers a threat - perhaps a boundary crossed, an injustice witnessed, a quiet disrespect in a meeting. Instantly, the sympathetic nervous system kicks in. Adrenaline and cortisol flood the bloodstream. The heart rate accelerates. Blood is shunted away from the digestive organs and toward the large muscles of the limbs. The body is preparing to fight.

This is where the trouble begins for so many women. We feel this surge of energy, this mobilization of the fascia and the musculature, and we immediately judge it. We call it "bad." We call it "unspiritual." We try to push it down, to swallow it, to smile through it. But energy does not simply disappear because we refuse to acknowledge it. It gets stored. It gets lodged in the levator scapulae, creating chronic neck pain. It gets trapped in the diaphragm, restricting our breath. It settles into the gut, disrupting our digestion.

As Gabor Maté has extensively documented in his work on the mind-body connection, the repression of healthy anger is a significant factor in the development of chronic illness, particularly autoimmune conditions. When the immune system is confused by the constant suppression of the fight response, it can begin to attack the body itself. Maté writes, "When we have been prevented from learning how to say no, our bodies may end up saying it for us." The body's refusal to be compliant is not a pathology; it is a desperate attempt at self-preservation.

## The Goddess in the Fascia

When we invoke Kali or Durga, we are not necessarily calling upon external deities to come and save us. We are recognizing the archetypal energies that already exist within our own neurobiology. Kali is the destructive force that clears away what is dead and false. She is the sudden, terrifying clarity that arises when we finally say, "No more." Durga is the sustained, focused power that protects what is sacred. She is the stamina required to hold a boundary over time.

These energies live in the body. They live in the deep core, in the psoas muscle that connects the spine to the legs, the muscle that initiates the fight-or-flight response. When we allow ourselves to feel our anger without judgment, we are allowing the psoas to do its job. We are allowing the energy to move through us, rather than getting stuck.

I remember a client who came to me with chronic jaw pain. She had tried everything - night guards, massage, acupuncture. But she was also in a marriage where she was constantly undermined, her reality subtly denied. We didn't start by trying to relax her jaw. We started by acknowledging the rage that was clamped down in her masseter. We gave it permission to be there. We invoked the energy of Kali, the fierce protector. And slowly, as she began to allow the anger to surface, the jaw began to release. The tension was not the problem; the tension was the holding back of the solution.

## The Wisdom of the Somatic Lineage

We are not the first to grapple with this. The somatic lineages have always known that the body is the crucible of transformation. Resmaa Menakem, in his groundbreaking work on trauma and the body, speaks of "clean pain" versus "dirty pain." Clean pain is the pain that mends and builds your capacity for growth. It is the pain of feeling your anger, of setting the boundary, of having the difficult conversation. Dirty pain is the pain of avoidance, of suppression, of pretending everything is fine when it is not.

Menakem teaches that we must build the capacity in our nervous systems to hold the charge of clean pain. This means learning to tolerate the physical sensations of anger - the heat, the trembling, the tightness - without immediately trying to discharge it or suppress it. It means staying with the body, tracking the sensations, and allowing the energy to complete its cycle.

This is where the herbs come in. We do not use herbs to numb the anger or to force the body into a false state of calm. We use them to support the nervous system as it does this heavy lifting. A strong infusion of [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) nourishes the adrenals, providing the deep mineral support needed when the sympathetic nervous system has been working overtime. [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) is the classic remedy for the anxious, fluttering heart that often accompanies unexpressed rage. It does not suppress the emotion; it simply gives the heart the courage to hold it.

## The Practice of Fierce Presence

How do we actually do this? How do we invite Kali and Durga into our daily lives, into our actual bodies?

It begins with a shift in orientation. Instead of trying to get rid of the anger, we turn toward it. We become curious about it. We ask it what it knows.

Here is a short embodied practice for when the rage arises:

Find a place where you can be alone and uninterrupted. Stand with your feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent. Feel the ground beneath you. Notice the sensations in your body. Where is the anger living right now? Is it in the chest? The throat? The jaw? The belly?

Place your hands on that area. Do not try to change the sensation. Just offer it your presence. Breathe into the space under your hands.

Now, allow your body to make the shape of the anger. If the anger had a posture, what would it be? Perhaps your hands curl into fists. Perhaps your jaw juts forward. Perhaps you crouch down, ready to spring. Let your body take the shape, and hold it. Feel the power in it. Feel the protection in it.

Stay with this shape for as long as you need. Notice what happens to the energy. Does it shift? Does it move? Does it change into something else?

When you are ready, slowly release the posture. Shake out your arms and legs. Take a deep breath, and let it out with a sigh.

## The Alchemy of Digestion

Anger, like food, must be digested. If we swallow it whole, it rots in the gut. If we chew it, break it down, and assimilate its nutrients, it becomes fuel. The digestive fire, the agni of Ayurvedic medicine, is not just for breaking down rice and lentils. It is for breaking down experience.

When we feel the heat of anger, we are feeling the activation of this digestive fire. It is trying to process a boundary violation, an injustice, a betrayal. If we suppress the heat, the experience remains undigested. It becomes *ama*, toxic residue that clogs the physical and energetic channels.

This is why the suppression of anger so often manifests as digestive issues - IBS, acid reflux, chronic bloating. The gut is literally holding the undigested emotion. The enteric nervous system, the "second brain" in the gut, is intimately connected to our emotional state. When we deny our anger, we are confusing the signals between the brain and the gut.

To digest anger, we must allow the heat to burn. We must feel the fire in the belly. We must let it do its purifying work. This is the energy of Durga, the invincible one, who rides the lion and carries the weapons of truth. She does not shy away from the battle. She meets it head-on, with clear eyes and a steady heart.

## The Legacy of the Good Girl

So many of us were raised to be "good girls." We were taught that our value lay in our compliance, our pleasantness, our ability to make others comfortable. We were taught that anger was ugly, unladylike, and dangerous.

This conditioning runs deep. It is wired into our neural pathways. When we begin to reclaim our anger, we will inevitably encounter the fear of losing love, of being abandoned, of being seen as "too much."

This is where the practice requires immense self-compassion. We must hold the frightened "good girl" with one hand, while wielding the sword of Kali with the other. We must remind ourselves, over and over again, that our anger is not a threat to our worthiness. It is a testament to our vitality.

Bessel van der Kolk, in *The Body Keeps the Score*, writes about how trauma robs us of our agency, our ability to take action to protect ourselves. Reclaiming our anger is a profound act of reclaiming our agency. It is the body saying, "I am here. I matter. I will not be erased."

When we allow ourselves to feel the full spectrum of our emotions, including the fierce, protective rage of the goddess, we become whole. We become dangerous, in the best possible way. We become women who cannot be easily manipulated, controlled, or silenced.

## The Integration

The goal is not to walk around in a constant state of rage. The goal is fluidity. The goal is a nervous system that can mobilize into fight-or-flight when necessary, and then return to a state of rest and digest.

When we stop fighting our anger, it stops fighting us. It becomes a trusted advisor, a clear signal that something requires our attention. We can feel the heat rise, acknowledge the message, take the necessary action, and then let the energy dissipate.

This is the true spiritual path. It is not a path of bypassing the messy, difficult realities of human life. It is a path of descending into the body, of meeting whatever arises with fierce presence and unwavering compassion. It is the path of Kali and Durga, the path of the awakened feminine, roaring her truth into the world.

## FAQ

**Q: I feel like if I let myself be angry, I'll never stop. It feels like a bottomless well. What do I do?**
**A:** This is a very common fear, especially for women who have suppressed their anger for decades. The body is holding a backlog of unprocessed energy. The key is titration - taking it in small doses. You don't have to feel it all at once. Start by just noticing the physical sensations of irritation or frustration. Track the heat in your chest or the tension in your jaw for just a few seconds, and then consciously shift your attention to something neutral or comforting. Build your capacity slowly. The well is not bottomless; it just hasn't been drained in a long time.

**Q: How do I express my anger without being abusive or destructive to the people around me?**
**A:** There is a profound difference between feeling anger and acting it out. You can feel the full, raging force of Kali in your own body without ever raising your voice or saying a cruel word. The practice is to separate the *feeling* from the *behavior*. Feel the somatic reality of the anger completely - let the energy move through your fascia, your muscles, your breath. Once the physical charge has been processed, you will have the clarity to communicate your boundary or your truth firmly, without the need to attack the other person.

**Q: I've been told that anger causes disease and that I need to forgive to heal. Is this true?**
**A:** The *suppression* of anger causes disease. The chronic, low-grade stress of holding down your natural protective responses wreaks havoc on the immune and endocrine systems. Feeling your anger cleanly and allowing it to move through you is actually deeply healing. As for forgiveness, it cannot be forced or rushed. Premature forgiveness is just another form of spiritual bypassing. True forgiveness often only arises naturally *after* the anger has been fully felt, honored, and digested.

**Q: What herbs can I use when I feel completely overwhelmed by rage?**
**A:** When the sympathetic nervous system is stuck in overdrive, we need nervines that ground and center without suppressing. [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20) is excellent for the circular, obsessive thoughts that often accompany anger. Blue Vervain is specific for the person who holds their tension in their neck and shoulders and feels rigidly attached to their own rightness. And as mentioned, Motherwort is the great ally for the heart that feels battered by its own fury.

**Q: Is it really okay to be angry at spiritual teachers or systems that taught me to suppress my feelings?**
**A:** Absolutely. In fact, it is necessary. Many spiritual communities have weaponized the concepts of "peace" and "love" to maintain control and silence dissent. Feeling the anger at having your reality denied or your boundaries crossed in the name of "spirituality" is a vital step in reclaiming your own somatic authority. Your body's truth is the ultimate guru.

## The Deeper Layers of the Fascia

When we talk about the body holding anger, we must look at the fascia - the continuous web of connective tissue that wraps every muscle, bone, nerve, and organ. The fascia is not just structural; it is highly innervated and deeply responsive to our emotional state. When we suppress the urge to fight back, the fascia literally tightens, creating a physical armor.

This armoring is a brilliant survival strategy in the short term. It numbs us to the pain and keeps us functioning. But over years and decades, this fascial restriction begins to pull the skeleton out of alignment. It compresses the organs. It restricts the flow of blood and lymph. The anger we refused to feel becomes the chronic stiffness we cannot stretch away.

To release this, we cannot force it. The fascia responds to slow, sustained pressure and to safety. When we finally create a safe container to feel our rage, the fascia begins to melt. The armor dissolves, not because we attacked it, but because it is no longer needed. The body realizes the war is over, or at least, that we are finally willing to fight it consciously.

## The Role of the Breath

Notice what happens to your breath when you are angry. It becomes shallow, high in the chest. The diaphragm, the primary muscle of respiration, locks up. This is the body preparing for action - short, quick breaths to fuel the muscles. But when the action is thwarted, when we swallow the anger, the diaphragm stays locked.

We walk around chronically under-breathing. This sends a continuous signal to the nervous system that we are in danger, keeping us in a state of low-grade sympathetic arousal. To break this cycle, we must address the diaphragm. We must breathe into the belly, not to calm down and pretend we aren't angry, but to give the anger the oxygen it needs to burn cleanly.

Deep, diaphragmatic breathing while holding the awareness of anger is a profound practice. It says to the body: "I feel the threat, I feel the rage, and I am safe enough to breathe through it." This is the integration of Kali's fire with the deep, grounding waters of the earth.

## The Ancestral Inheritance

We must also acknowledge that the anger we carry is not always entirely our own. We carry the epigenetic imprint of our ancestors - the women who were burned, silenced, institutionalized, or simply ground down by a world that demanded their submission.

When you feel a rage that seems disproportionate to the current situation, you may be tapping into this ancestral well. This is not a pathology; it is a profound sensitivity. You are feeling the accumulated grief and fury of your lineage.

Honoring this ancestral anger requires a different kind of holding. It requires ritual. It requires acknowledging that you are the one in the lineage who finally has the safety and the resources to feel what could not be felt before. You are doing the metabolic work for your ancestors. This is sacred work. It is the work of Durga, restoring balance to the world.

## The Courage to Be Disliked

Ultimately, reclaiming your anger means relinquishing the need to be universally liked. It means accepting that your boundaries will inconvenience people. Your truth will make people uncomfortable. Your "no" will disappoint those who benefited from your "yes."

This is the price of admission to your own life. You can either be liked by everyone else, or you can be at peace with your own body. You cannot have both.

When you choose your body, you align yourself with the fierce deities. You become a force of nature. You become a woman who knows her own power, who trusts her own sensations, and who refuses to apologize for the space she takes up in the world. This is the ultimate spiritual permission.
